name: Bhatta Ramakantha II
dates_notes: Mature dualist Saiddhāntika commentator active in Kashmir contemporaneously with Abhinavagupta; dated by his commentaries' references to Sadyojyotis (predecessor) and by his standing as the figure Abhinavagupta's school must answer.

lineage_polemical.note: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha's Nareśvara-Parīkṣā-Prakāśa is the principal mature dualist Saiddhāntika reply to the Pratyabhijñā position, defending the eternal real distinction of soul from Śiva against Utpaladeva's identity-recognition thesis.

Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha and Abhinavagupta are the two great Kashmirian Śaiva theologians of the late tenth century, working at the same place and moment but defending incompatible metaphysical commitments — eternal pluralism versus non-dual self-recognition.
Rāmakaṇṭha's polemic targets the Pratyabhijñā lineage Somānanda founded; the Nareśvara-Parīkṣā-Prakāśa engages the school's analysis of self-awareness and its non-dual conclusions.

core_thesis: Non-Vedāntin comparator (mature dualist Kashmir Śaiva-Siddhānta). Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha II works in Kashmir at the same time and place as Abhinavagupta, writing *vyākhyā* on the Saiddhāntika scriptural canon and mounting a sustained *bhedavāda*-defence against the *advaya-vāda* of the rising Pratyabhijñā lineage. He receives Sadyojyotis's three-fold ontology of *pati*, *paśu*, and *pāśa* and re-states it under the new dialectical pressure of Buddhist epistemology and Pratyabhijñā idealism (*Nareśvara-Parīkṣā-Prakāśa* on *NP* 1.1–25, the foundational reception). His distinctive contribution is a rigorous account of the soul's *sva-saṃvedana* — its intrinsic, non-illuminated self-awareness — as belonging to the soul *itself*, not as a borrowed mode of a single divine consciousness (*NPP* on *NP* 1.30–55, the *sva-saṃvedana* argument; *Mataṅga-Vṛtti* on *Mataṅga-Pārameśvara* *Vidyā-pāda* 6, the parallel locus). The soul is eternally a discrete, conscious *cit*-substance bound by *āṇavamala* (*Kiraṇa-Vṛtti* on *Kiraṇa-Tantra* *Vidyā-pāda* 1.16–22); Śiva is eternally the supreme Lord (*NPP* on *NP* 2.1–40); and *māyā* is a real material principle (*Mataṅga-Vṛtti* on *MP* *Vidyā-pāda* 13, on *māyā-tattva*). Against Utpaladeva's *vimarśa* read as Śiva's universal self-awareness (*ĪPK* 1.5.10–17), Rāmakaṇṭha argues that self-awareness is a property of every individual soul taken as itself a substance (*NPP* on *NP* 1.30–55). The polemical work is performed in his *Nareśvara-Parīkṣā-Prakāśa* and in his major scriptural commentaries on the *Mataṅga-Pārameśvara* and *Kiraṇa* tantras.

engaged_works.title: Matanga-Vrtti
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on the Mataṅga-Pārameśvara-Tantra; the principal Saiddhāntika scriptural commentary of its period.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus (N. R. Bhatt and Hélène Brunner editions; Watson and Goodall studies).

engaged_works.summary: The major commentary on the *Mataṅga-Pārameśvara-Tantra* — one of the cardinal scriptures of the Śaiva-Siddhānta tradition. The *Mataṅga* itself is a long Saiddhāntika tantra organised into *Vidyā-pāda* (philosophical doctrine), *Kriyā-pāda* (ritual), *Yoga-pāda* (yoga), and *Caryā-pāda* (conduct). Rāmakaṇṭha's *Vṛtti*, which survives in substantial form especially on the *Vidyā-pāda*, develops the dualist Saiddhāntika doctrine to its fullest commentarial articulation: the three-fold ontology of *pati / paśu / pāśa* as eternally real and irreducible (*Mataṅga-Vṛtti* on *MP* *Vidyā-pāda* 1–3); the *tattva*-cosmology read through a realist rather than emanationist lens (*Mataṅga-Vṛtti* on *MP* *Vidyā-pāda* 11–17, on the *tattva-mārga*); the soul's *sva-saṃvedana* as belonging to the soul itself (*Mataṅga-Vṛtti* on *MP* *Vidyā-pāda* 6, the parallel to *NPP* on *NP* 1.30–55); the *malas* as actual ontological obscurations rather than cognitive errors (*Mataṅga-Vṛtti* on *MP* *Vidyā-pāda* 7–9); and the necessity of Śiva-bestowed *dīkṣā* for liberation (*Mataṅga-Vṛtti* on *MP* *Vidyā-pāda* 25–26). The work is one of the principal sources for reconstructing the mature dualist Saiddhāntika system, the natural counterweight to Abhinavagupta's *Tantrāloka* in any structural account of the Kashmirian Śaiva field.

engaged_works.title: Kirana-Vrtti
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on the Kiraṇa-Tantra.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus (Goodall 1998 critical edition of Vidyā-pāda chapters 1–6).

engaged_works.summary: The principal commentary on the *Kiraṇa-Tantra*'s *Vidyā-pāda* — one of the most-studied scriptures of the Śaiva-Siddhānta. The text is structurally a dialogue between the sage Garuḍa and Śiva on the nature of the bound soul, the bonds, and Śiva, and Rāmakaṇṭha's *Vṛtti* uses each *pāda* to articulate the school's philosophical position. Notable features include Rāmakaṇṭha's defence of the soul as *eternally* conscious (*nitya-cit*), against any reading on which the soul's consciousness is borrowed or derivative (*Kiraṇa-Vṛtti* on *Kiraṇa-Tantra* *Vidyā-pāda* 1.16–22; on *KT* 3.16–25 the regress argument against *jñāna-santāna*); his account of *āṇavamala* as a real obscuring substance that requires real removal (*Kiraṇa-Vṛtti* on *KT* *Vidyā-pāda* 2.1–25); his treatment of *karma* and its fruits as ontologically real conditions of *bhoga* (*Kiraṇa-Vṛtti* on *KT* *Vidyā-pāda* 5); and his arguments for the necessity of an omniscient supreme Śiva as the *prerakṛ* — the agent who, through grace and *dīkṣā*, conjoins soul and *māyā* for the soul's purification (*Kiraṇa-Vṛtti* on *KT* *Vidyā-pāda* 6). The Goodall 1998 critical edition of the first six chapters has reset the field's understanding of the Kashmirian dualist Saiddhāntika in the late tenth century.

engaged_works.title: Naresvara-Pariksa-Prakasa
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on Sadyojyotis's Nareśvara-Parīkṣā; the principal mature dualist Saiddhāntika reply to the Pratyabhijñā non-dualism.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus (Watson 2006, The Self's Awareness of Itself).

engaged_works.summary: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha's commentary on Sadyojyotis's *Nareśvara-Parīkṣā* and his most significant philosophical work in its own right. The *Prakāśa* is structurally a *vṛtti*, but it is in effect an extended polemical treatise: Rāmakaṇṭha uses each of Sadyojyotis's verses as the occasion for a developed reply to the major rivals of late tenth-century Kashmir, with the rising Pratyabhijñā lineage of Utpaladeva and Abhinavagupta as the principal target (*NPP* on *NP* 1.1–25, the foundational disposition; *NPP* on *NP* 1.30–55 directly engaging *ĪPK* 1.5.10–17). The central polemical argument concerns *sva-saṃvedana*: against the Pratyabhijñā doctrine that self-awareness (*vimarśa*) is the property of a single supreme consciousness whose contractions appear as bound subjects, Rāmakaṇṭha defends the position that *sva-saṃvedana* belongs intrinsically to each soul *as a substance* (*NPP* on *NP* 1.30–55). The argument has two prongs: a regress argument against any mediated account of self-awareness (*NPP* on *NP* 1.40–48), and a phenomenological argument that the unity of the experiential field is best explained by an enduring substantial self rather than by contraction-of-supreme-Self (*NPP* on *NP* 1.49–55, and *NPP* on *NP* 3.1–40 on the eternal distinction at *mukti*). The work is the high-water mark of the dualist Kashmirian theological tradition; Alex Watson's 2006 monograph (*The Self's Awareness of Itself*) is the definitive modern study.

engaged_works.title: Sardha-Tri-Shatika-Lokulisvaragama-Vrtti
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on the Lokulīśvarāgama (Lakulīśvarāgama) in three hundred and fifty verses; the standard dualist Saiddhāntika commentary on this Pāśupata-adjacent scripture.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the *Lokulīśvarāgama* — a three-hundred-and-fifty-verse Śaiva scripture standing within the broader Pāśupata-adjacent Atimārga / Mantramārga interface that Rāmakaṇṭha reads firmly within the Saiddhāntika frame (*Sārdha-Tri-Śatika-Vṛtti* on *Lokulīśvarāgama* opening section, the foundational disposition). The *Vṛtti* extends the dualist position into the territory of *yoga-praxis* and the soteriological role of *dīkṣā*: each chapter is read so as to confirm that the soul's purification is an objective ontological transformation (*STSV* on *Lokulīśvarāgama* sections on *karma-mala* and its *kṣaya*), that *Śiva*'s grace operates through ritual rather than through identity-recognition (*STSV* on the *dīkṣā*-related sections), and that the path to liberation runs through the school's *dīkṣā*-system rather than through gnostic insight (*STSV* on the concluding *mukti*-sections). Read together with the *Mataṅga-Vṛtti* and *Kiraṇa-Vṛtti*, the work establishes the breadth of Rāmakaṇṭha's commentarial range across the Saiddhāntika scriptural canon. Less philosophically ambitious than the *Nareśvara-Parīkṣā-Prakāśa*, but central to the school's living ritual tradition.

reception_notes: Rāmakaṇṭha is the great mature dualist Saiddhāntika theologian of Kashmir; his polemic against the Pratyabhijñā position is the principal philosophical alternative to the non-dualism that subsequently dominates the popular reception of Kashmir Śaivism. The modern recovery of his work — through Goodall's editions of the Kiraṇa, through Watson's monograph on the Nareśvara-Parīkṣā-Prakāśa, and through ongoing work in the École Française d'Extrême-Orient corpus — has made the dualist Saiddhāntika position visible as a serious metaphysical alternative within Kashmirian Śaivism, not as a 'preliminary' stage to the non-dual Trika.
